rationale: The citing paper discusses Wansink and Chandon (2006) in the theory section as concrete evidence that simplified verbal claims (e.g., "low-fat") can backfire by increasing consumption, even reporting the approximate 50% effect. It also uses that study in framing why consumers may avoid nutrition processing in hedonic categories due to guilt-related dynamics. This is more than a passing mention because it helps motivate the paper's core problem and conceptual distinction between claim-based labels and comprehensive POS nutrition scores.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Wansink and Chandon (2006) as prior empirical support that simple nutrition claims can mislead consumption behavior. That evidence serves as a conceptual foundation for testing whether a more comprehensive POS scoring system can improve food choices and how effects vary by category context.
